nodes.title: Return early for empty tree
nodes.why: An empty tree is already valid, so we avoid unnecessary traversal work.
nodes.alternative: Always run traversal and rely on null checks inside recursion.
nodes.tradeoff: One quick guard line for cleaner control flow.

nodes.title: Use inorder traversal to expose sorted order
nodes.why: Inorder on a valid BST is sorted, so swapped values become visible as inversions.
nodes.alternative: Collect all values first, then sort and compare arrays.
nodes.tradeoff: Recursive traversal is simple and O(h) stack, without extra arrays.

nodes.title: Detect where inorder order breaks
nodes.why: When prev.val > current.val, we found a misplaced pair in the sequence.
nodes.alternative: Try local left/right child comparisons only.
nodes.tradeoff: Global inorder check is reliable; local checks can miss cross-subtree swaps.

nodes.title: Capture first offender once, update second as needed
nodes.why: This handles both adjacent and non-adjacent swaps with one pass.
nodes.alternative: Stop after first inversion and swap immediately.
nodes.tradeoff: Slightly more state tracking, but correct for all swap patterns.

nodes.title: Swap the two detected values in place
nodes.why: Only values changed, so the tree structure stays intact.
nodes.alternative: Rebuild a new tree from sorted values.
nodes.tradeoff: In-place swap is minimal and keeps node references unchanged.
